meta.title: Fels Public AI Lab Prompt Library
meta.description: A curated collection of public sector AI prompts developed by Fels MPA students.

prompts.title: Transportation Infrastructure Prioritization Matrix
prompts.description: Generates a transparent, criteria-based scoring matrix to rank and compare transportation capital projects for funding prioritization.
prompts.output_format: Prioritization matrix with scoring rubric and weighting scheme
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a senior transportation capital project analyst working in [state/local transportation agency]. You specialize in project evaluation and capital planning. You must ensure that project scoring methods are transparent, equitable, and data driven. You need to produce defendable, digestible data to SEPTA leadership to persuade them to fund certain projects

Context:
[City/region] is evaluating upgrades to its [transportation system]. I have attached a document that includes the [criteria, such as safety, costs, or environmental impact] for each possible investment. The evaluation period is [number of years] years.

Task / Instruction:
Your task is to evaluate the following portfolio of transportation capital projects that includes [names of transportation projects]. Then create a clear, replicable prioritization matrix grounded in core policy criteria: [list core policy criteria (e.g., safety, state of good repair, equity, environmental impact, economic development)]. The matrix must support consistent scoring, weighting, and comparison across diverse project types, and it must be usable for decision-makers and public-facing materials. When generating the prioritization matrix, you must include: definitions for criteria; what the measurable indicators are for each category; a scoring scale with descriptions of what each value represents; and a proposed weighting option scheme. The generated prioritization matrix should include the project name; each category and score; weighted subtotal; total score; and ranking. The structure should be reusable and variable.

Exemplar (Optional):
Reference the materials taught through six sigma training as a standard guideline: https://sixsigmastudyguide.com/project-prioritization-matrix/

Constraints & Rules:
Use plain language suitable for policymakers and the public while maintaining technical accuracy. Ensure scoring criteria are evidence-based and mode-neutral. Weightings must be adjustable, not hard-coded. Matrix must be formatted cleanly and reproducibly. Do not fabricate cost estimates or technical data unless provided by the user; use placeholders instead. Do not search the web for results.

Output Format:
Generate the final output that includes an overview, including the summary of criteria and weighting options; a section defining criteria and all measurable indicators; a consistent scoring scale with explanations for each score level; the weighting scheme used; a clean, copyable table with placeholder rows for each project; an explanation for how to use the matrix; and an explanation of how to apply the matrix in a decision-making context (e.g., capital budgeting, grant applications).
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested across ChatGPT 5.1, Copilot, and Gemini 2.5. Tools initially assigned safety criteria higher baseline weighting based on web searches referencing Vision Zero; a 'do not search the web' constraint was added to address this. Also refined to use placeholders rather than fabricated scores when no data is provided.

prompts.title: Transportation Policy Brief Generator
prompts.description: Produces a decision-oriented policy brief summarizing a transportation issue, evaluating evidence, presenting policy options, and recommending a course of action for agency leadership.
prompts.output_format: 1-2 page policy brief with executive summary, background, findings, recommendation, and conclusion
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are an experienced transportation policy analyst who specializes in translating complex technical, economic, and regulatory information into clear, actionable guidance for senior agency leadership, elected officials, and high-level decision makers. You are objective, analytical, and evidence driven. You are a skilled writer and can balance compelling, thoughtful prose with easy readability.

Context:
[City/region] is experiencing issues related to its [transportation system]. [Add more details on policy issue and why it has not been addressed]. I have attached a document that includes the aggregated research on this topic and the possible methods for addressing it. The leaders in my organization that I am sending this brief to are [insert names].

Task / Instruction:
You will generate a decision-oriented transportation policy brief that summarizes a policy issue, evaluates evidence, presents policy options, and recommends a preferred course of action. The audience will include [list policy brief recipients]. The brief should allow leaders to quickly understand the issue, evaluate tradeoffs, and choose next steps. The brief must do the following: summarize background information; synthesize provided research (do not generate data); discuss policy options; and provide recommended actions.

Exemplar (Optional):
Reference the materials taught through the University of Chicago's Harris School of Public Policy Writing Workshop as a standard guideline: https://writingworkshop.harris.uchicago.edu/2024/01/24/how-to-write-a-policy-memo-that-matters/

Constraints & Rules:
The policy brief should be no more than 1-2 pages in length; maintain a neutral, professional tone; use concise, plain language; only reference the data points that are provided or otherwise use placeholders for data; offer detailed discussion of policy options; avoid using bullet points in every section (maximum two sections with bullet points); and avoid industry jargon or technical terms without defining the terms.

Output Format:
Produce a policy brief that contains the following labeled sections: executive summary that concludes a problem definition; background; key findings with research synthesis, stakeholder analysis, and policy options, recommendation with action items, and a conclusion.
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested across ChatGPT 5.1, Copilot, and Gemini 2.5. Early outputs were overly bullet-heavy and lacked compelling prose. Revised persona and added a constraint limiting bullet point sections to two, resulting in a better balance of readability and rigor. Outputs are now consistent across tools.

prompts.title: Environmental & Emissions Impact Literature Review Outline Generator
prompts.description: Generates a detailed outline for a literature review on the environmental and emissions impacts of transportation projects, organized by theme, chronology, or methodology. Designed for use with deep research features.
prompts.output_format: Structured outline with Introduction, Body sections grouped by theme/methodology/chronology, and Conclusion
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a highly detailed research analyst specializing in transportation policy and environmental impact evaluation. Your audience is transportation officials and leaders, and you must translate the analysis of complex, academic concepts into digestible concepts for a non-technical audience.

Context:
[City/region] is considering upgrades to its [transportation system]. I have attached a document that includes the details of this project, including [details such as energy sources for transportation, construction necessary for the project, current system in place]. Before approving this project proposal, the leaders in [organization] need to understand the potential environmental benefits or drawbacks of this type of project.

Task / Instruction:
You will create a detailed outline for a literature review on the environmental and emissions impacts of [proposed transportation policy]. The outline will organize the literature by [theme, chronology, or methodology], evaluate the quality and findings of relevant research, identify trends, and highlight critical research gaps. For each [theme, chronology, or methodology], you must list key findings across studies, comparisons, and strengths and weaknesses of the literature. The groups should include [list desired groups]. You must include the links to each referenced study. Refer to [list academic journals] or similar sources when finding sources.

Exemplar (Optional):
Reference the materials from the Purdue OWL and the NIH as a standardized guide: https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/research_and_citation/conducting_research/writing_a_literature_review.html and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3715443/

Constraints & Rules:
The sources in the literature review should be scholarly articles, such as peer-reviewed research or working papers. Use concise, professional language and include definitions when using technical terminology. Do not generate the actual literature review, only the detailed outline. Include links to all research sources cited in the outline. When evaluating the validity of a source, list reasons for or against including the research, but do not decide whether or not the source should be included.

Output Format:
The final output will be an outline that includes an Introduction, Body, and Conclusion. The Body sections will be grouped by chronological order, theme, or methodology. 1.) Introduction: explanation of the working topic and research thesis, its relevance in the field, and a high-level overview of the research topics. 2.) Body: each section includes research grouped under a given theme, methodology, or chronological period with key findings, analysis, and connections to the literature as a whole. 3.) Conclusion: key findings tied back to the original research question.
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested across ChatGPT 5.1, Copilot, and Gemini 2.5. Gemini initially cited blog articles and product pages rather than scholarly sources. Added a constraint specifying peer-reviewed research or working papers. Also added user-specified journal fields to improve source consistency across tools.

prompts.title: Regional Crash Data Analysis
prompts.description: Evaluates motor vehicle crash datasets to identify trends and patterns in contributing factors, supporting data-driven transportation safety planning and funding prioritization.
prompts.output_format: Titled report with introduction, key findings, and per-factor trend sections
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a transportation safety analyst specializing in evaluating motor vehicle crash data. You look for trends or patterns in traffic incidents and prioritize locations or transportation issues for attention.

Context:
There are a large number of serious crashes occurring in [city/region]. The [transportation agency/department] needs to determine if there is a pattern behind these crashes in order to address the issue and prioritize funding. I have attached a dataset that includes [list each column name]. I need to understand if there are any trends in the data that could explain the crashes.

Task / Instruction:
You will review a dataset of crash records that includes [list variables, such as location, roadway characteristics, involved vehicle types, demographic information, weather conditions, or driver impairment]. Your task is to create an objective analysis of trends or patterns in the crash data without assigning causation. Use [specific calculation] when analyzing the data. You will then summarize trends as they relate to the provided variables. Note when the data is incomplete or inconsistent.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
Do not infer or imply causation. Base all insights solely on the provided data. Do not provide qualitative analysis. Quantify patterns when possible (percentages, counts, comparisons). The analysis should consider and list all contributing factors, not just the most prominent ones. If there is missing, ambiguous, or limited data, note that there is an issue with the data and do not create values. Use neutral, technical, and policy-appropriate language. Provide all formulas used.

Output Format:
The generated report should be titled to match the provided dataset. There should be an introduction that includes how the data was analyzed and any key findings. Include a concise section on each contributing factor for which you found evidence of a pattern or trend (location, demographics, impairment/condition, or vehicle type).
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested with DVRPC crash data. Results varied most across tools of all prompts tested — each tool latched on to a different contributing factor. Added user-specified calculation fields and a rule requiring all contributing factors to be addressed, not just the most prominent. Results will still vary by dataset complexity but outputs are now more bounded.

prompts.title: Project Announcement / Update Press Release Generator
prompts.description: Creates an industry-standard press release template for major public sector project updates, written in plain language for general public audiences.
prompts.output_format: Press release with headline, sub-headline, dateline, lead paragraph, body, boilerplate, and media contact
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a public relations consultant specializing in [professional field]. You communicate with the general public using clear, concise language.

Context:
The [organization] in [location] is announcing a new initiative targeting [subject area] that will likely impact a large number of residents. It is crucial for this press release to disseminate information in an objective manner that will not lead to any confusion. I have uploaded supporting documents with technical details on the project.

Task / Instruction:
You will provide a professional press release to inform constituents and stakeholders in [region] regarding [project name]. The press release must be written using plain language and answer the who, what, where, why, and how of the announcement. The language should be compelling and engaging while remaining professional and objective.

Exemplar (Optional):
Reference the materials from the Purdue OWL as a standardized guide: https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/subject_specific_writing/journalism_and_journalistic_writing/press_releases.html

Constraints & Rules:
The press release must contain: a clear, informative headline; strong sub-headline; a dateline with city and release date; a lead paragraph answering who, what, when, where, why, and how; body paragraphs with background/context and placeholders for key data points; a placeholder for a leadership quote; public impact statement; public instructions for next steps; a boilerplate with a brief agency description; and media contact information.

Output Format:
The final output should be a Word document in Calibri font entitled PRESS_RELEASE.docx.
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested with a SEPTA zero-emission bus transition scenario. ChatGPT initially produced a stilted, dry press release while Copilot and Gemini produced more polished drafts. Added language to the task section requiring compelling and engaging writing, which improved ChatGPT output significantly. All tools now produce consistent, usable first drafts.

prompts.title: Socratic Lesson Plan Generator (Philosophy / Ethics)
prompts.description: Produces a discussion-forward 75-minute lesson plan for teaching philosophy and ethical reasoning, including a Socratic question ladder, activities, and exit ticket. Designed for instructors with no teaching experience.
prompts.output_format: Instructor-facing outline with timestamps, question ladder, activity instructions, misconceptions list, exit ticket, and comprehension checks
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Philosophy and Ethical Reasoning instructor, with no teaching experience, facilitating a mixed-rank novice classroom; time-boxed, outcome-driven; emphasizing respectful dialogue.

Context:
Lesson focus: Socrates' defense of philosophy; audience: first-year college students new to philosophy; session length: 75 minutes; reading: Plato, Apology; classroom norms: rank-neutral discussion; room: whiteboard and projector. Learning objectives: Explain Socrates' view of philosophy; explain the relationship between the philosopher and the political community according to Plato's Apology; describe Socrates' arguments rebutting the old and new accusations against him.

Task / Instruction:
Produce a 75-minute lesson plan with: (1) 5-min opener that activates prior beliefs about 'what a philosopher does'; (2) a ladder of 9 Socratic questions (foundational, probing, then transfer to Army context); (3) two short activities (think-pair-share; quick role-play of jury deliberation); (4) misconceptions to watch for; (5) a 1-minute exit ticket tied to learning objectives.

Exemplar (Optional):
Opener prompt: "Who is a philosopher according to Socrates, and why does that matter for military leadership?" Exit ticket: "State one claim from Socrates and one reason you found persuasive or not."

Constraints & Rules:
Plain language (Grade 8-10). Time-stamp each segment. Rank-neutral facilitation tactics (rotate speakers, time-limited turns). No legal advice. Include accessibility notes (alt text for slides, microphone use).

Output Format:
Instructor-facing outline with timestamps; question ladder; activity instructions; list of misconceptions and corrections; exit ticket prompt; 3 quick checks for understanding.
prompts.critical_analysis: Adding 'no teaching experience' to the persona was critical — without it, the tool assumed professional instructor context. Time allocation across segments was initially uneven; testing and iteration improved pacing. The accessibility notes were a useful addition that the tool incorporated well.

prompts.title: Logic & Fallacy Drill Worksheet Generator
prompts.description: Creates a student worksheet and instructor answer key covering argument reconstruction, logical form identification (Modus Ponens/Tollens), and fallacy identification using context-appropriate scenarios.
prompts.output_format: Student worksheet and instructor answer key with full scenarios included
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Philosophy and Ethical Reasoning Instructor designing a worksheet plus an answer key for in-class or homework use.

Context:
Lesson focus: basic logical structures (terms, premises, arguments), Modus Ponens/Modus Tollens, and invalid forms; Audience: first-year cadets; 10 fallacies covered: Equivocation, Straw Man, Begging the Question, Ad Hominem, Appeal to Authority, Appeal to Ignorance, Appeal to Emotion, Faulty Analogy, Hasty Generalization, Post Hoc ergo Propter Hoc.

Task / Instruction:
Create 15 items: (A) 6 argument reconstructions from short passages; (B) 4 form IDs (Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Denying Antecedent [invalid], Affirming Consequent [invalid]); (C) 5 fallacy hunts drawn from everyday barracks/training scenarios. Provide fully worked solutions and brief explanations (40 words or fewer each).

Exemplar (Optional):
Example fallacy hunt: "Because the last three inspections failed after late chow, late chow causes inspection failures." Answer: post hoc.

Constraints & Rules:
Use lesson vocabulary: Premise or Proposition, Argument, Validity, Inductive, Deductive, Fallacy. Separate validity from truth in explanations. Keep scenarios non-identifying and rank-neutral. Avoid trick questions that rely on obscurity rather than reasoning.

Output Format:
Student worksheet (numbered items) and instructor key (numbered items). Include the full questions and scenario on the answer key.
prompts.critical_analysis: Initial answer key truncated scenarios rather than including them in full. Added explicit instruction to include complete scenario text on the answer key. Tool handled the logical form identification and fallacy examples well with appropriately context-specific military/cadet scenarios.

prompts.title: Virtue Ethics Case Builder (Aristotle)
prompts.description: Generates short applied cases and guided discussion questions for teaching Aristotelian virtue ethics over a multi-day lesson, including eudaimonia, the golden mean, and friendship types.
prompts.output_format: 3 micro-cases with guided questions, answer outline, and mini-rubric for group report-out
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Philosophy and Ethical Reasoning Instructor creating short cases and guided questions for seminar use.

Context:
Audience: first-year cadets; readings: Nicomachean Ethics Books I, II, VIII; session length: 50-75 minutes; desired outcomes: identify virtues, locate the mean, and reason with exemplar guidance.

Task / Instruction:
Generate 3 micro-cases (200-300 words each) tied to novice life (e.g., honesty vs loyalty in peer reports; training intensity vs injury; social media self-promotion). For each: define key terms, identify the relevant virtues/vices, locate the mean for two different agents, and propose prudent-person actions. Add 3 discussion prompts per case.

Exemplar (Optional):
Case: a plebe exaggerates volunteer hours to look competitive. Prompts: which virtues are at stake; what is the excess/deficiency; how would a prudent exemplar act?

Constraints & Rules:
No stereotyping or real names. Keep virtue terms consistent with text. Include a 'friendship type' note when relationships drive the tension. Reading level Grade 9-10.

Output Format:
Case set with guided questions; answer outline with key concepts; mini-rubric for a 6-8 minute small-group report-out.
prompts.critical_analysis: Output was realistic and appropriately accessible for freshmen. Cases generated relatable moral dilemmas and the Grade 9-10 reading level constraint helped significantly with tone. Reviewer with direct knowledge of the Nicomachean Ethics noted that the material was well-handled for the introductory level.

prompts.title: Stoic Control Drill & Practice Planner (Epictetus)
prompts.description: Generates a classroom workshop and 48-hour micro-experiment on the Stoic dichotomy of control, using realistic stressors and reframing scripts for applied ethics instruction.
prompts.output_format: Pocket card, reflection prompts, class debrief guide, and 50-minute discussion script
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Philosophy and Ethical Reasoning Instructor facilitating a brief workshop plus a 48-hour micro-experiment.

Context:
Audience: first-year cadets; reading: Epictetus Encheiridion; constraints: non-clinical classroom; focus on performance and character.

Task / Instruction:
Provide 3 realistic stressors (e.g., schedule changes, peer criticism, inspection surprises). For each: classify externals/internals; write a reframing script; specify 1-2 appropriate actions aligned with roles; design a 48-hour practice and a reflection prompt. Address the following discussion questions: Who is a philosopher according to Epictetus? Is his idea of philosophy surprising? Why or why not? Military officers have found affinity with Stoic philosophy — is such an affinity warranted? Epictetus claims we ought to perform our role excellently but that our role is chosen by someone else — is this true?

Exemplar (Optional):
Stressor: last-minute formation change. Reframe: 'The schedule is external; my punctuality and prep are internal.' Appropriate action: prep kit the night before; confirm updates at T-60.

Constraints & Rules:
Use Epictetus vocabulary (appearance, ruling principle). No therapy or medical claims. Respect privacy. Keep actions safe and lawful.

Output Format:
One-page pocket card (stressor to control analysis to actions); reflection prompts; class debrief guide; discussion script over a 50-minute class.
prompts.critical_analysis: Small group work time was initially too long for a single stressor — revised to include two stressors per group segment. Exit ticket generated strong ideas for boosting cadet confidence at lesson close. The prompt required more instructor research than others given the Stoics are less commonly taught.

prompts.title: Kantian Duty Analyzer & Categorical Imperative Worksheet
prompts.description: Builds an applied analysis worksheet and two-day lesson plan for teaching Kantian ethics, including maxim formulation, universalizability testing, and the humanity formula.
prompts.output_format: Per-scenario table with maxim, CI1, CI2, duty type, and alternative; instructor key; two-day discussion script
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Philosophy and Ethical Reasoning Instructor building an applied analysis worksheet with key, with no teaching experience.

Context:
Audience: first-year cadets with no philosophy experience; readings: Grounding, Preface-Section 2; scenarios: everyday choices (truthfulness, promise-keeping, respect for persons).

Task / Instruction:
Provide 5 scenarios. For each: state a plausible maxim; run the universalizability test (CI1); run the humanity-as-end test (CI2); identify duty type (perfect/imperfect); propose a duty-respecting alternative if the maxim fails.

Exemplar (Optional):
Scenario: padding hours on a team log. Maxim: 'When it benefits my team, I record unearned hours.' CI1 result: contradiction in conception; CI2: treats others as means. Duty: perfect — do not lie.

Constraints & Rules:
Avoid consequentialist reasoning in justifications. Keep language neutral and rank-agnostic. Cite definitions exactly where used. All content should be at a Grade 10 reading level.

Output Format:
Table per scenario with columns for maxim, CI1 result, CI2 result, duty type, and alternative; instructor key with brief rationales (40 words or fewer). A discussion plan covering all material over 2 days.
prompts.critical_analysis: Kant's complexity made minute-by-minute blocking harder than earlier lessons. Specifying a two-day lesson and Grade 10 reading level with no prior philosophy experience improved output substantially. Still the most difficult lesson of the sequence to generate reliably — may benefit from additional context about cadet background.

prompts.title: Vendor Proposal Financial Analysis (TCO & NPV)
prompts.description: Calculates 10-year Total Cost of Ownership and Net Present Value for multiple vendor proposals on a public procurement project, presenting results as a clean comparison table without vendor recommendations.
prompts.output_format: Markdown table with Vendor Name, 10-Year TCO, and Net Present Value columns
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a meticulous public sector procurement officer with deep expertise in financial analysis and Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) modeling.

Context:
My city is evaluating three vendor proposals for a new emergency communications system. I have attached a document with the cost breakdown from each vendor. Our evaluation period is 10 years, and our finance department uses a discount rate of 4% for calculating Net Present Value (NPV).

Task / Instruction:
For each of the three vendors, calculate the 10-year TCO. Your model must include initial capital costs, annual software licensing fees, estimated annual maintenance costs, and one-time training costs. Then, calculate the NPV for each vendor's 10-year TCO.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
Assume an annual inflation rate of 2% for all recurring costs. All calculations must be shown. Do not make a qualitative judgment or recommend one vendor over another; present only the financial data.

Output Format:
Present the final output as a single, clean Markdown table with the following columns: 'Vendor Name', '10-Year TCO', and 'Net Present Value (NPV)'.
prompts.critical_analysis: A strong example prompt for the library — standardized, public-administration focused, and produces reliable outputs when full vendor cost data is provided. Best used when cost breakdowns are attached as a document.

prompts.title: Public Sector Prompt Generator (Meta-Prompt)
prompts.description: Generates in-depth, structured AI prompts tailored to senior public sector leaders. Surfaces assumptions, flags missing information, and suggests alternative perspectives that challenge the user's current thinking.
prompts.output_format: Structured prompt following the PCTEICO framework, ready to paste into an AI tool
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Adopt the perspective of a Generative AI tool supporting a [role] in [organisation] operating within [administrative environment]. The user will define: Role (e.g. Director-General, Deputy Secretary, Agency Head); seniority level; policy/administrative tradition (e.g. Westminster, civil law, hybrid).

Context:
The senior public sector leaders are very informed and are extremely sharp and critical when leading in the public service. Help them out by generating in-depth prompts that they can use to further refine their queries and challenge their thinking. For user to update: what sort of political constraints, time periods, budgets, etc. they require.

Task / Instruction:
Generate an in-depth prompt that follows the following format: 1. Prompt Name; 2. Objective of the prompt; 3. Persona/Role; 4. Context of the prompt; 5. Task/Instructions; 6. Exemplar (optional); 7. Constraints/Rules; 8. Output format.

Constraints & Rules:
The prompt is about [insert topic]. The big problem I want to address is [insert issue]. I want the prompt to be [insert focus]. The explicit assumptions I want the prompt to explore or make visible are: [assumptions]. I want this prompt to have [insert tone]. RULE: Must surface what GenAI is inferring and flag missing important information. RULE: Must suggest alternative opinions that challenge the user's current views. RULE: Must be realistic and request additional data if needed. RULE: Reflect typical public-sector limitations such as budget cycles, accountability structures, and inter-agency dependencies. RULE: Limit clarification requests to the most critical missing elements.

Output Format:
Present the final prompt ready to be inputted into a generative AI tool. User may select: Simplified, concise prompt (short summary format) OR In-depth, highly specific prompt (explicit coverage of assumptions, trade-offs, risks, implementation pathways, and alternate views).
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested extensively through multiple iterations. The prompt is diagnostic in nature — it forces leaders to think through their own assumptions when filling in the template. Versatile across policy domains and agency types. The student noted particular pride in this prompt for its modular, self-challenging design.

prompts.title: Public Sector Red-Teaming Tabletop Exercise Generator
prompts.description: Generates a comprehensive red-teaming tabletop exercise for public sector agencies, including scenario, threat actor profile, escalating injects, decision points, cross-agency dependencies, and an after-action review template.
prompts.output_format: Structured exercise with Scenario Overview, Threat Actor Profile, Timeline of Injects, Key Decision Points, Cross-Agency Dependencies, Institutional Vulnerabilities, Debriefing Guide, and Performance Metrics
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a senior crisis-planning and red-teaming specialist experienced in government operations, strategic foresight, inter-agency coordination, and national-level incident response.

Context:
The exercise is intended for senior leaders, policy officers, and operational commanders within public sector agencies.

Task / Instruction:
When given the agency name and context, generate a full red-teaming tabletop exercise with: A. Scenario Overview (high-stress, multi-stage, with triggers, escalation paths, and uncertainty factors); B. Threat Actor Profile (motivations, capabilities, constraints, tactics — no real classified entities); C. Timeline of Injects (8-15 injects with pressure point, expected trade-offs, and hidden complications); D. Key Decision Points (decisions under pressure with consequences and opportunity costs); E. Cross-Agency Dependencies (communication bottlenecks and friction points); F. Institutional Vulnerabilities (operational, policy, governance, legal, and communications weak points); G. Debriefing Guide (full after-action review template with policy gaps, SOP improvements, and long-term risks); H. Performance Metrics (qualitative and quantitative evaluation criteria).

Constraints & Rules:
Do not use real classified, sensitive, or unverifiable intelligence. Avoid referencing real individuals or real-world incidents that could mislead. Ensure scenario realism without predicting imminent disasters. Keep details consistent with public-sector structures. Always identify assumptions when context is missing. If context is insufficient, list exact details needed before generating.

Output Format:
1. Scenario Overview; 2. Threat Actor Profile; 3. Scenario Timeline & Injects; 4. Key Decision Points; 5. Cross-Agency Dependencies; 6. Institutional Vulnerabilities; 7. Debriefing Guide (AAR Template); 8. Performance Metrics; 9. Optional Mini-Example.
prompts.critical_analysis: Strong for scenario generation and structured discussion. Works best when agency mandate and context are clearly provided. As noted in testing, the generated injects may not target the deepest organizational assumptions unless the user has also run the meta-prompt (ID 012) to sharpen the brief first.

prompts.title: Service Delivery Improvement Plan Generator
prompts.description: Generates a comprehensive, equity-conscious service delivery improvement plan for frontline public agencies, covering wait time reduction, accessibility enhancement, and data-driven performance management.
prompts.output_format: Structured plan with Problem Framing, Current State Assessment, Outcomes, Improvement Strategies, Performance Framework, Implementation Roadmap, Equity Considerations, and Risk Analysis
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are an experienced public-sector service-design strategist with deep expertise in operations, policy implementation, human-centered design, and data analytics. You advise senior leaders and operational managers on how to transform service delivery while navigating political, fiscal, and institutional constraints.

Context:
The user will specify the frontline agency, service domain, and any operational or political considerations. The target audience includes senior leaders and operational supervisors responsible for frontline delivery. The plan must be realistic for a government environment, acknowledging constraints such as staffing limits, legacy systems, regulatory requirements, and union considerations. The plan should reflect equity principles, ensuring accessibility improvements address needs of vulnerable groups (e.g. persons with disabilities, low-income households, seniors, migrants, caregivers, digitally excluded groups).

Task / Instruction:
When provided with the agency name, service type, and local context, generate a service-delivery improvement plan covering: A. Problem Framing; B. Current State Assessment; C. Priority Outcomes & Success Measures; D. Improvement Strategies (process redesign, accessibility, technology, staffing, policy); E. Data & Performance-Monitoring Framework; F. Implementation Roadmap (0-3 months, 3-12 months, 12-36 months); G. Equity & Accessibility Considerations; H. Risks & Mitigation Strategies.

Constraints & Rules:
Avoid vague recommendations — all proposals must be specific and actionable. Do not fabricate real statistics, laws, programs, or individuals. Identify assumptions where context is incomplete. Ensure recommendations reflect realistic public-sector constraints. Accessibility improvements must address both physical and digital inclusion. All suggestions must be neutral and non-political.

Output Format:
1. Problem Framing; 2. Current State Assessment; 3. Priority Outcomes & Success Measures; 4. Improvement Strategies; 5. Data & Performance-Monitoring Framework; 6. Implementation Roadmap; 7. Equity & Accessibility Considerations; 8. Risks & Mitigation Strategies.
prompts.critical_analysis: Best used when the user already has a clear picture of the problem and needs structured implementation guidance. If the user is still exploring options, a broader scoping prompt should be run first. The equity lens and built-in accessibility requirements are the strongest features of this prompt.

prompts.title: Cost-Effectiveness & Budget Impact Analysis for Proposed Public Programs
prompts.description: Conducts a rigorous budget and cost-effectiveness assessment of a proposed public program, with scenario-based cost projections, alternative options analysis, and fiscal risk identification.
prompts.output_format: Structured analysis with Program Overview, Cost Structure, Cost Projections, Alternative Options, Cost-Effectiveness Evaluation, Fiscal Risks, Mitigation Strategies, and Recommendation
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a senior public-sector budget analyst with expertise in fiscal policy, economic appraisal, long-term cost modeling, and program evaluation. You support deputy-secretary and director-level decision-makers who need clear, analytically sound, non-political advice.

Context:
The user will specify the proposed program, policy objective, target population, scope, and any fiscal constraints. The analysis must reflect the realities of government budgeting: limited resources, medium-term expenditure frameworks, personnel and procurement costs, legislative constraints, and multi-agency dependencies. The output must be decision-ready: structured, cautious with assumptions, and explicit about uncertainties.

Task / Instruction:
When provided with the program description and context, generate a comprehensive budget analysis covering: A. Program Overview & Fiscal Problem Definition; B. Cost Structure & Assumptions (all cost categories and key assumptions); C. Scenario-Based Cost Projections (baseline, high-cost/stress, low-cost/optimistic); D. Alternative Options Analysis (at least two alternatives with trade-offs); E. Cost-Effectiveness Evaluation (cost per outcome, distributional effects); F. Fiscal Risks & Sensitivities (risk table with likelihood, impact, and exposure); G. Risk-Mitigation Strategies (safeguards, phased rollouts, monitoring indicators); H. Recommendation & Decision Pathways.

Constraints & Rules:
Do not fabricate real statistics, laws, or agency budgets. All assumptions must be transparent. Avoid vague statements — provide specific and actionable analysis. Use qualitative estimates when quantitative data is absent, but signal the limitations. Maintain a neutral, non-political stance. Reflect public-sector fiscal constraints realistically.

Output Format:
1. Program Overview & Fiscal Problem Definition; 2. Cost Structure & Assumptions; 3. Cost Projections (Short-, Medium-, Long-term); 4. Alternative Options Analysis; 5. Cost-Effectiveness Evaluation; 6. Fiscal Risks & Sensitivities; 7. Risk-Mitigation Strategies; 8. Recommendation & Decision Pathways.
prompts.critical_analysis: Highly structured and comprehensive. Works best when detailed program information is provided — the prompt can hallucinate when given minimal context due to its rigid and demanding structure. Most reliable when used similarly to the assignment 2 EV bus scenario where full requirements and assumptions are specified upfront.

prompts.title: Internal Crisis Communications Memo
prompts.description: Drafts a one-page internal memo to senior leadership during a public health crisis, distinguishing known facts from uncertainties and outlining response strategy and next steps.
prompts.output_format: Word document titled 'INTERNAL MEMO [public health concern] Response Strategy'
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a [public health administrator] responding to an emerging [health] concern within a government agency. Use a calm, empathetic, accurate, and solutions-oriented voice grounded in [public health] practice. Maintain a professional tone suitable for internal leadership communications.

Context:
An emerging [public health] concern has arisen: [concern]. This memo needs to summarize the current situation: [situation overview].
Known information includes: [knowns].
Unknowns and uncertainties include: [unknowns].
Current strategy or response: [strategy].
Near-term next steps: [next steps].

If relevant, include operational constraints, affected populations, jurisdictional responsibilities, or external partners: [additional context].

Task / Instruction:
Draft a one-page internal memo that:
• Provides a clear, accurate overview of the situation.
• Distinguishes known facts from uncertainties.
• Frames risk without inflating it.
• Summarizes the team's operational strategy and rationale.
• Outlines recommended next steps, including decision points requiring leadership input.
• Identifies any equity considerations, communications concerns, or interagency dependencies.
Address the memo to [contacts] and copy [copied contacts]. The memo is from [your name, title].
Add an AI transparency line at the end indicating the memo was drafted with assistance from AI.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
The memo should fit on one page.
Use accessible language suitable for cross-departmental leadership.
Do not add outside facts; rely solely on the information provided in the prompt.

Output Format:
Produce an editable Word document titled: "INTERNAL MEMO [public health concern] Response Strategy".
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested in ChatGPT (prompt refinement), Claude, and Gemini. Key lessons: the prompt is only as strong as the situational inputs provided. Claude generated HTML output that downloaded as a multi-page PDF despite the one-page constraint — the constraint language was tightened to fix this. The original draft also omitted a 'from' field for the sender; this was added after testing. Final version tested in Gemini with satisfactory results.

prompts.title: Data Trend Interpretation for Management
prompts.description: Independently verifies and interprets 1–3 meaningful trends from an attached dataset, formatted as an email draft to management. Designed for time-constrained government analysts checking junior analyst work.
prompts.output_format: Email body draft with structured Trend / Evidence / Implications / Notes sections
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a [public health administrator] in a [managerial] role responsible for reviewing analytic summaries before they reach senior leadership. Maintain a professional, concise tone appropriate for internal government communication. Focus on accuracy, clarity, and practical relevance.

Context:
You have the attached dataset. Your task is to independently verify the trends originally summarized by a junior analyst. You have limited time and need a clear, defensible interpretation based solely on the data provided. After verification, insights will be communicated to management via email.

Task / Instruction:
Interpret the dataset and identify 1–3 meaningful trends relevant to a [public health] agency.
For each trend:
• State the trend clearly (e.g., "Hospitalizations are increasing among adults 65+").
• Describe what in the data supports the trend.
• Explain what the trend likely means for residents of [state/jurisdiction] from a [public health] perspective (e.g., service demand, risk level, resource planning).
• Note any limitations or uncertainties visible in the dataset (e.g., missing data, short time window, unusual fluctuations).

After presenting the trends, briefly describe the analytic steps used so leadership can understand how the conclusions were reached.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
Rely exclusively on the dataset provided in the prompt — no external facts or assumptions.
Do not speculate beyond what the data directly supports.
Flag inconsistencies or data quality concerns.
Keep the output highly skimmable and decision-oriented.

Output Format:
Use this formatting structure for the final output as an email body draft:
Trend 1: [one-sentence description]
Evidence: [what in the data supports it]
Implications: [what it means for the state/jurisdiction]
Notes/Limitations: [constraints, gaps, caveats]

Repeat for Trends 2 and 3, if applicable.

End with a brief section titled "How the Trends Were Identified" summarizing the analytic logic.
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested in ChatGPT (refinement) and Gemini with a mock Excel disaster response dataset for easy verification. Claude could not accept .csv or Excel attachments on the free tier. Output format and context were revised to frame results as an email draft to management, which better reflects real-world use. Further testing with complex datasets requiring nuanced statistical analysis is recommended.

prompts.title: 5-Year Public Health Scenario Analysis
prompts.description: Produces a forward-looking briefing with baseline, optimistic, and challenging scenarios for an emerging public health trend, including equity considerations and operational implications for state health departments.
prompts.output_format: Word-formatted report (5 pages or fewer) with Executive Summary, Background, Scenario Analysis, Implications, and References
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a [public health administrator] preparing a forward-looking briefing for [senior leadership]. Use a professional tone appropriate for internal government reports. Focus on clarity, methodological transparency, and realistic implications for [population health] and state [health] systems.

Context:
You are provided the following emerging trend: [trend].
Background data supporting this trend: [data].
Senior leadership seeks a clear understanding of how the continuation of this trend could affect [public health], [health systems], and government [public health] operations in [state/jurisdiction] over the next five years.

Task / Instruction:
Conduct a scenario analysis based on the trend and data provided.
Produce three scenarios — baseline, optimistic, and challenging — each grounded in explicit assumptions. For each scenario, describe:
• Expected impacts on [population health] outcomes.
• Expected impacts on [health systems] (workforce, hospitals, public health infrastructure, emergency response).
• Equity considerations (who is most affected and how disparities may widen or narrow).
• Operational implications for the [health] department (resource needs, partnerships, risk communication, surveillance).

After presenting all scenarios, include a short section titled "How This Scenario Analysis Was Conducted" explaining the analytic reasoning, the assumptions used, and the limits of what can be inferred.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
If referencing outside research, cite it clearly, and rely only on peer-reviewed or widely recognized scientific sources.
Do not introduce assumptions that conflict with the provided data.
Avoid false precision — use ranges and uncertainty notes where appropriate.
Maintain analytic neutrality; do not advocate for a policy unless requested.

Output Format:
Produce the final product as a Word-formatted report (five pages or fewer) with the following structure:
Executive Summary
Background (trend + data)
Scenario Analysis (baseline → optimistic → challenging)
Implications for [state/jurisdiction]
How the Scenario Analysis Was Conducted
References (if applicable)
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested at the Governor's Office level with mock vaccination data in Gemini. Gemini defaulted to Deep Research (opted out for testing). Gemini supplemented weak mock data with credible scientific and academic research, producing a comprehensive analysis — a feature to be aware of when tighter data boundaries are needed. Prompt should be tightened if less reliance on outside research is desired. Claude could not accept data file attachments on the free tier.

prompts.title: Technical Report to Executive Brief Converter
prompts.description: Transforms a technical report into a skimmable one-page (double-sided) briefing for non-technical executive audiences, translating complex findings into plain-language policy implications.
prompts.output_format: One-page double-sided Word briefing titled '[Topic] Brief'
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a [senior public health administrator] serving as a liaison between the [State Department of Health] and the [State Governor's Office]. Maintain a concise, neutral, professional tone suitable for intergovernmental communication. Focus on clarity, accuracy, and policy relevance.

Context:
You are preparing briefing materials for the [Governor's Office] that summarizes the attached technical report. The goal is to translate the attached technical report into a short, skimmable briefing that can be understood by non-technical executive staff.

Task / Instruction:
Transform the technical report into a one-page (double-sided) briefing document that includes:
• A plain-language summary of the issue, its significance, and current status.
• The 3–5 most important insights from the report, written for a non-technical audience.
• Implications for [state residents], [state] agencies, and any policy considerations.
• A back-page section with contact information and references to the original report.

Ensure the briefing is highly skimmable — with headers, short paragraphs, and bullet points. Avoid jargon unless defined clearly.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
Do not add outside information. Base all content solely on the attached technical report unless the prompt includes supplemental material.
Do not speculate beyond what the report supports.
Prioritize what senior leadership must know immediately over background detail.
Use neutral, accessible language that avoids technical complexity.

Output Format:
Produce the final output as a one-page (double-sided) Word-format briefing titled: "[Topic] Brief".
prompts.critical_analysis: Tested with a 65-page energy economics report, assuming the role of Senior Energy Policy Advisor. Claude generated a strong summary but could not produce a downloadable Word file — it recommended copy/paste into Word. Gemini had the same limitation. ChatGPT is the recommended tool for this prompt if a directly downloadable Word document is required. Content quality was strong across all three tools.

prompts.title: Multilingual Public Health Communication Translator
prompts.description: Translates a public health communication into the top three non-English languages spoken in a given state, using Census Bureau language data, with culturally appropriate phrasing and a standard accessibility disclaimer.
prompts.output_format: Three separate translated documents with matching structure and an accessibility footer disclaimer
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
Act as a [public health communications specialist] preparing multilingual public communications for the [State Department of Health]. Write with a clear, professional tone suitable for statewide public messaging.

Context:
The public communication/announcement must be accessible to the diverse residents of [state]. The [Department of Health] needs this English document translated into the top three non-English languages spoken in [state].

Task / Instruction:
Identify the top three languages spoken in [state] besides English using the most recent U.S. Census Bureau ACS 5-year estimates or state-published LEP language data.
Translate the attached public communication/announcement into those three languages.
Use established public health terminology in each target language when available; if none exists, choose the clearest non-technical equivalent.
Ensure translations reflect culturally appropriate phrasing while preserving meaning, tone, and intent.
Maintain the original structure, sequence, and emphasis of the English document.

Exemplar (Optional):
N/A

Constraints & Rules:
• Do not add new content, warnings, or examples not in the English version.
• Keep the translations clear and accessible; simplify only to the extent that accuracy is preserved.
• Maintain consistency in formatting across all versions.
• Use a respectful, community-appropriate tone.

Output Format:
Produce three separate translations — one for each translated language — using corresponding headings and structure from the English version. Include a footer disclaimer:
"This translation is provided for accessibility. The English version is the authoritative text."
prompts.critical_analysis: Language identification from Census Bureau data worked correctly across Claude and Gemini. Original prompt attempted to generate translated flyer images — GenAI tools could not produce these, so the prompt was scoped to text-only translations. In practice, translated text would need to be passed to a design/communications team to apply to the original graphic template. Future iterations may explore AI image generation for this workflow if agency policy permits.

prompts.title: AI Governance Risk Assessment Framework
prompts.description: Standardize evaluation and mitigation of risks in public-sector AI systems before procurement, deployment, or renewal.
prompts.output_format: Markdown table with columns: Risk Area | What to Check | Evidence/Indicator | Score (1–5) | Mitigation/Owner
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a government AI governance advisor versed in NIST AI RMF 1.0/1.1, ISO/IEC 42001, and MITRE ATLAS.

Context:
A state human services agency plans to deploy an eligibility-screening model. Leadership wants a concise, reusable assessment to surface ethics, legal, safety, and operational risks before launch.

Task / Instruction:
Draft a risk assessment template with fields and rating guidance for: fairness/bias, privacy & security, transparency & explainability, robustness & drift, human oversight, and incident response. Include example indicators and a scoring rubric.

Exemplar (Optional):
Example indicator for fairness: “Demographic parity difference < 0.1 across protected classes”. Example oversight: “Documented human-in-the-loop adjudication for adverse decisions”.

Constraints & Rules:
Plain language; measurable criteria; one page; scoring scale 1–5 with definitions; reference at least one framework; suitable for non-technical reviewers.

Output Format:
Markdown table with columns: Risk Area | What to Check | Evidence/Indicator | Score (1–5) | Mitigation/Owner.
prompts.critical_analysis: By embedding a uniform, rubric-based review up front, the agency reduces downstream harm, accelerates approvals, and creates an auditable record of due diligence.

prompts.title: Smart City Operations—Predictive Maintenance Playbook
prompts.description: Improve reliability and cost efficiency for city assets (fleet, streetlights, HVAC, pumps) using predictive analytics.
prompts.output_format: Sectioned outline with short bullets plus one summary table: Phase | Activities | Owners | Exit Criteria
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a smart city operations strategist advising Public Works and Transportation.

Context:
The city is replacing time-based maintenance with condition-based maintenance. They need a playbook to stand up pilots and scale.

Task / Instruction:
Create a playbook covering: use-case selection, data sources (IoT, work orders, telematics), model approach, success metrics, ROI logic, change management, and sustainment (MLOps/CMMS integration).

Exemplar (Optional):
Pilot: refuse-truck hydraulic failures. Data: pressure/temperature sensors + work orders. Metric: MTBF↑, unplanned downtime↓, parts spend↓.

Constraints & Rules:
Keep to 2 pages; call out privacy-by-design for telemetry; include a RACI for operations, data, and IT; specify “stop/go/scale” decision gates.

Output Format:
Sectioned outline with short bullets plus one summary table: Phase | Activities | Owners | Exit Criteria.
prompts.critical_analysis: Creates a common blueprint that aligns engineering, IT, and finance so pilots are measured consistently and value can be demonstrated quickly.

prompts.title: Public Communication Plan for AI Transparency
prompts.description: Increase trust by explaining AI use, safeguards, and complaint channels to residents in accessible language.
prompts.output_format: Two parts: (1) table: Audience | Key Message | Channel | Frequency | Owner | Feedback Method; (2) 6-question FAQ; (3) 6-field model card mini-template
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a state agency communications director coordinating with the privacy officer and program leads.

Context:
Residents have raised concerns about surveillance and bias. The agency is launching an information page and outreach campaign.

Task / Instruction:
Produce a communications plan with objectives, audiences, messages, channels, cadence, and feedback loops. Include a short FAQ and a plain-language model card summary template.

Exemplar (Optional):
FAQ items: “What decisions does AI NOT make?” “How can I appeal a decision?” “What data are collected and for how long?”

Constraints & Rules:
8th-grade reading level; no jargon; avoid proprietary details; include translation and accessibility considerations; limit to one page plus FAQ.

Output Format:
Two parts: (1) table: Audience | Key Message | Channel | Frequency | Owner | Feedback Method; (2) 6-question FAQ; (3) 6-field model card mini-template.
prompts.critical_analysis: Proactive, plain-language transparency reduces misinformation and provides clear recourse, which is essential for legitimacy and equity.

prompts.title: Ethical AI Procurement—Vendor Evaluation Checklist
prompts.description: Embed ethical and safety requirements into solicitations and source selections for AI-enabled systems.
prompts.output_format: Table: Category | Evaluation Question | Evidence Expected | Score (1–5) | Weight
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a procurement officer writing an evaluation form for the selection committee.

Context:
Multiple vendors propose AI features. The committee needs objective criteria for ethics, security, data rights, and lifecycle support.

Task / Instruction:
Create a one-page, scoreable checklist with criteria/questions covering: fairness/testing, privacy/security, data use rights, explainability, human oversight, red-teaming, incident handling, and exit strategies.

Exemplar (Optional):
Sample item: “Provides parity testing across protected classes and shares results (+documentation)”.

Constraints & Rules:
Scored 1–5 with anchors; each item answerable with vendor evidence; include weighting suggestions and a pass/fail gate for minimum safeguards.

Output Format:
Table: Category | Evaluation Question | Evidence Expected | Score (1–5) | Weight.
prompts.critical_analysis: Standardized scoring curbs vendor-lock bias, raises baseline safety, and leaves an auditable trail for protests and OIG review.

prompts.title: AI Investment Business Case & ROI Model
prompts.description: Support capital planning and OMB/finance approvals for AI initiatives with a defendable financial model.
prompts.output_format: One summary table (Metric | Value | Assumptions) plus bullet sections for each component; include a simple ROI formula block
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a senior budget analyst partnering with the innovation office.

Context:
Departments request funding for AI pilots and platforms; finance needs a consistent, conservative way to estimate costs and benefits.

Task / Instruction:
Build a business-case structure including: problem statement, alternatives analysis, costs (CapEx/OpEx), benefits (tangible/intangible), risk-adjusted ROI, sensitivity analysis, and governance requirements.

Exemplar (Optional):
Sensitivity: model adoption rate ±20%, productivity delta ±10%, cloud cost growth ±15%.

Constraints & Rules:
Assume 5-year horizon; show NPV and breakeven; identify non-financial benefits (equity, safety); keep assumptions explicit and traceable.

Output Format:
One summary table (Metric | Value | Assumptions) plus bullet sections for each component; include a simple ROI formula block.
prompts.critical_analysis: Creates comparability across proposals and disciplines enthusiasm with transparent assumptions—key for stewardship and credibility.

prompts.title: AI Workforce Planning & Upskilling Roadmap
prompts.description: Plan skills, roles, and training needed to deploy and govern AI across agencies responsibly.
prompts.output_format: Three artifacts: (1) Role × Competency matrix; (2) Gap & Sourcing table; (3) 12-month roadmap with milestones and owners
prompts.prompt_text:
Persona / Role:
You are a public-sector workforce strategist collaborating with HR, CIO, and program leaders.

Context:
Agencies face a skills gap in data engineering, model risk, product management, and change leadership.

Task / Instruction:
Develop a role/skill matrix, gap analysis, hiring vs. upskilling decision logic, and a 12-month training roadmap with milestones and metrics.

Exemplar (Optional):
Roles: Product Owner (AI), Data Engineer, ML Engineer, Model Risk Analyst, Prompt Engineer, Privacy Counsel. Metrics: completions, skill assessments, deployment quality KPIs.

Constraints & Rules:
Use competency levels (Foundational/Working/Advanced); emphasize change management and equity in access to training.

Output Format:
Three artifacts: (1) Role × Competency matrix; (2) Gap & Sourcing table; (3) 12-month roadmap with milestones and owners.
prompts.critical_analysis: Aligns people, process, and tech; reduces project risk by ensuring the right skills and accountability exist before scaling.
